# The Hidden Fitness of the Male Zebra Finch Courtship Song

## Summary

Vocal learning in songbirds is thought to have evolved through sexual selection, with female preference driving males to develop large and varied song repertoires[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#R1)- [3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#R3). Yet, many songbirds learn only a single song in their lifetime[4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#R4). How sexual selection drives the evolution of single song repertoires is not known. Here, using dimensionality reduction techniques applied to singing behavior, we show that syllable spread in low-dimensional feature space explains how single songs function as honest indicators of fitness. We find that this gestalt measure of behavior captures spectral distinctiveness of song syllables, that females strongly prefer songs that occupy more latent space, and that matching path lengths in low-dimensional space is difficult for young males. Our findings clarify how simple vocal repertoires may have evolved in songbirds and indicate divergent strategies for how sexual selection can shape vocal learning.

* * *

Sexual selection is a powerful driving force in the evolution of ornamental phenotypes that signal fitness to potential mates[5](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#R5)- [7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#R7). This is particularly evident in songbirds, where female preference is theorized to underlie the evolution of remarkable vocal learning abilities, and large song repertoires in the males of many species[8](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#R8)- [13](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#R13). However, this model for the evolution of vocal learning does not adequately explain the vocal repertoires of all songbird species[14](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#R14). It is estimated that ~1,300 species, almost one-third of all oscine songbirds, only learn to produce a single song during their lifetimes[4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#R4). For this substantial group, it is poorly understood how vocal learning has been shaped by sexual selection and how their singular songs provide honest indicators of fitness supporting mate selection.

The single song of a zebra finch is characterized by the serial and stereotyped production of ~4–7 complex song syllables. The spectral and temporal organization of song is slowly achieved through aggregated changes in vocal patterns as young birds learn to imitate their father[15](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#R15)- [17](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#R17). As adults, male zebra finches use their song in courtship displays and female zebra finches have a strong preference for these imitated songs over songs produced by birds experimentally raised without access to an adult song tutor ( _improvised_ or untutored song, [Fig. 1a](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#F1))[17](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#R17)- [23](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#R23).

## Figure 1. High throughput analysis of imitated and improvised songs of the zebra finch.

![Figure 1.](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/0e83/11410162/34b263e62c79/nihms-2018754-f0001.jpg)

[Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/figure/F1/)

(a) Schematic of song learning in juvenile zebra finches. Juveniles learning from an adult tutor will produce an imitated song in adulthood. Juveniles raised in the absence of an adult tutor will produce an improvised song in adulthood. (b) Schematic of Deep Avian Network. Variance of amplitude and filtering is performed on raw audio recordings of individual zebra finch songs. Periods of sound are detected and extracted. Acoustic features of song are calculated on a sample of the detected sounds to form a training set for the neural network. The trained neural network is then applied to the entirety of the audio dataset (~2,000+ renditions of each syllable).

It is still unclear how imitated song may function as an honest indicator of male quality. Most studies have focused on examining female preference for individual features of song, like the number of song syllables or song duration, or manually selected acoustic parameters[24](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#R24)- [29](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#R29). However, these studies have returned mixed results (reviewed by[30](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#R30)). More recently it has been found that females readily recognize the song of individual birds regardless of how song syllables are rearranged, suggesting that zebra finches attend to yet unidentified _gestalt_ aspects of imitated songs[31](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#R31)- [33](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#R33).

Here, we developed and then tested a hypothesis for how zebra finch song can signal fitness. To explore what large scale or emergent song feature(s) females may attend to, we embedded half a million song syllables from adult male birds into low-dimensional latent feature-spaces. We then asked how imitated songs, which females prefer, differ from improvised songs. Our analysis identified the spread of syllables in two-dimensional Euclidean space as a robust feature distinguishing these classes of songs from one another. We next used behavioral experiments in female and male zebra finches to test whether this novel _gestalt_ metric for imitated song captures how a single song may serve as an honest indicator of male quality and support the evolution of a single song repertoire through sexual selection.

### Large-scale Analysis of Zebra Finch Song

We conducted a comprehensive study of adult zebra finch songs, with the goal of recording several thousand song renditions from each bird. A day's worth of audio recordings from a single bird contains many tens of thousands of calls, cage noises, and individual syllables that need to be segmented and categorized. To achieve this, we developed Deep Avian Network (DAN), a machine and deep learning pipeline to efficiently segment, filter, analyze, and classify sounds with minimal need for manual annotation ( [Fig. 1b](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#F1)). DAN first calculates the variance of the raw audio files, applying a gaussian filter to smooth fluctuations, and uses thresholding to reliably identify periods of silence and sound. Automated segmentation, based on the first derivative of the amplitude variance, then provides highly accurate identification of individual sounds ( [Extended Data Fig.1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#F5); the average duration of DAN segmented syllables and introductory notes is within <1.0 ms of hand labeled data).

The machine learning portion of DAN categorizes a small subset of segmented audio files to serve as a training set for the deep learning component of the pipeline. DAN calculates the root mean square of amplitude, entropy, pitch goodness, mean frequency, power spectrum density and pixel intensity of spectrograms of sounds from an individual bird. Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection (UMAP) based dimensionality reduction on the combined feature set is then used to identify similar audio segments[34](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#R34). A hierarchical clustering algorithm is applied to the embedded UMAP space to obtain clusters of highly similar sounds. The resulting clusters are visually inspected for incorrect assignments, and processed clusters are then fed into a Siamese-convolutional neural network (sCNN, [Extended Data Table 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#T1)). Once trained, the sCNN can generalize and quickly cluster the remaining segmented audio signals from each bird, resulting in a robust and efficient approach for analyzing the hundreds of thousands of vocal signals used in this research ( [Fig. 1b](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#F1), [Extended Data Fig.1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#F5)).

### Imitated and Improvised Songs

To search for global differences between imitated and improvised songs we performed dimensionality reduction on 135,855 DAN-segmented syllable spectrograms from 18 adult male zebra finches ( [Fig. 2a](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#F2)). We found that zebra finch song syllables form multiple discrete clusters in UMAP space[35](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#R35), [36](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#R36)(Silhouette score = 0.83 with K-means, 0.76 with HDBSCAN)[37](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#R37). We noticed that imitated and improvised syllables occupied largely non-overlapping areas of the UMAP projection ( [Extended Data Fig. 2a](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#F6)). This motivated us to ask if qualitative differences in the spatial distribution of song syllables were reliable. UMAP preserves local neighborhoods in the dataset at the expense of absolute distances[34](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#R34), [37](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#R37). This characteristic may make Euclidean distances between clusters in UMAP capricious. To empirically examine this, we first asked if the segregation of clusters of improvised and imitated syllables is consistent by iteratively recalculating latent space embeddings with random sampling of the song syllables from individual birds. We found that 80% or 12 of 15 UMAP embeddings showed clear visual segregation between imitated and improvised syllables ( [Extended Data Fig. 2b](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#F6)- [c](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#F6)). This suggests that although absolute position of a syllable cluster changes from embedding to embedding, the larger scale organization of syllable spectrogram clusters in UMAP latent feature space is stable.

### Figure 2. Imitated songs span longer paths in latent feature space than improvised songs.

![Figure 2.](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/0e83/11410162/291a590fe522/nihms-2018754-f0002.jpg)

[Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/figure/F2/)

(a) UMAP projection of syllables from 18 birds with spectrograms of representative syllables within clusters. Each color is a different bird. Circled syllables indicate the five syllables that comprise the song of one bird in this dataset. Inset, similarity scores of syllables illustrated by their spectrograms around the UMAP, shows that latent space does not strongly reflect acoustic similarity. Only the exact same sound returns self-similarity score of 100% and a score of 98% reflects similarity for a highly stereotyped song syllable. (b) The minimum path length of each bird is consistent over UMAP iterations (n = 18 birds, n = 15 UMAP iterations). (c) UMAP projection of syllables from 49 birds. (d) Distribution of imitated and improvised syllables across UMAP quadrants. (e) Minimum path length of imitated songs is longer than improvised songs (n = 31 birds with imitated songs and n = 18 birds with improvised songs; raw path length, two-tailed t-test, p = 0.008; path length normalized to the number of syllables in song, two-tailed t-test, p = 0.04; \*p<0.05, \*\*p<0.01, \*\*\*p<0.001). Box plots in panel b and e report median, 25th and 75th percentile, and whiskers mark the 10th and 90th percentiles with single data points either overlaid or on left of box plots.

To quantitatively examine the reliability of song spectrogram latent feature space, we calculated the minimal Euclidean path length between the syllable spectrogram clusters of each bird in our dataset. We found that the path length of each bird is remarkably stable across different UMAP embeddings and has a significantly smaller coefficient of variation than shuffled path lengths ( [Fig. 2b](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#F2)). Moreover, the relative proportion of each bird’s contribution to the total path length was also consistent across different embeddings of these syllable spectrograms ( [Extended Data Fig. 2d](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#F6)). Together, these findings indicate that UMAP provides consistent representation of entire zebra finch songs, and that path length distributions across songs of individual birds might be capable of providing insights into _gestalt_ aspects of this behavior.

We next compared the spread of syllable spectrogram clusters between imitated and improvised songs using the minimum path length for the song of each bird. We found that the imitated songs spread out more in latent feature space than improvised songs and that this finding is robust to different UMAP embedding parameters ( [Extended Data Fig. 2e](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#F6), [Extended Data Fig. 3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#F7)), raising the possibility that this could be a biologically meaningful difference in song types. To more rigorously examine this, we first added DAN-segmented syllable spectrograms from an additional 31 birds, bringing our dataset to 472,475 song syllables ( [Fig. 2c](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#F2), [Extended Data Fig. 4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#F8)). We found a positive correlation between the path lengths of the original 18 birds described above and their new path lengths in this considerably larger embedding space, further underscoring the stability of song path lengths across UMAP embeddings ( _p_ = 0.06, R2 = 0.242).

In this larger data space, we found that improvised syllable spectrogram clusters no longer grouped together ( [Fig. 2d](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#F2), [Extended Data Fig. 4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#F8)). Even when imitated and improvised syllables clusters were evenly distributed across embedding space, imitated zebra finch songs still spread out significantly more than improvised songs ( [Fig. 2e](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#F2)). Together, these findings suggest that the spread of song within latent space can provide a reliable measure of syllables that distinguishes improvised and imitated zebra finch song.

### Female Song Preference

To help understand if spread of song syllables in latent space is biologically relevant, we asked if female zebra finches can distinguish between songs of different path lengths and if they exhibit a consistent preference for short or long path length songs. We assessed female song preference in a large free flight T-maze arena ( [Fig. 3a](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#F3)). We designed pairs of synthetic songs from imitated syllables in our large UMAP space and used playback of these songs in place preference tests ( [Extended Data Fig. 5a](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#F9)- [d](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#F9), [Supplementary Audio 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#SD1)- [6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#SD6)). By controlling for several aspects of syllable spectral complexity and duration, we isolated path length as the distinguishing feature in the song pairs used in our place preference experiments ( [Fig. 3b](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#F3), [Extended Data Fig. 5e](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#F9)- [j](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#F9)).

### Figure 3. Female zebra finches strongly prefer long path length songs over shorter length songs.

![Figure 3.](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/0e83/11410162/7410c1688971/nihms-2018754-f0003.jpg)

[Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/figure/F3/)

(a) Schematic of song playback T-maze arena with tracking of bird position during song playback shown for one example bird. Orange shading indicates increased time spent at indicated locations. (b) Spectrograms of a synthetic song pair used in place preference tests. Three pairs of synthetic songs were generated and used in these experiments. (c) Time spent in the long path arm increased during song playback and decreased after song playback ceased (ANOVA followed by Tukey's post-hoc test, n = 13 birds, p < 0.001 for pre-trial versus song playback and p < 0.01 for song playback versus post-trial times). (d) Female zebra finches increased the time spent in the long path length arm and decreased the time spent in the short path length arm (ANOVA followed by Tukey's post-hoc test, n = 13 birds, p < 0.001 for long path length arm versus home arm and short path length arm). Box plots report median, 25th and 75th percentile, and whiskers mark the 10th and 90th percentiles with single data points on the left.

We found that female zebra finches reliably distinguished between songs based on their spread, and that they strongly preferred longer over shorter path length songs ( [Fig. 3c](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#F3)- [d](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#F3), [Extended Data Fig. 6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#F10)). This finding held independent of the arm in which we played the song, the underlying baseline arm preferences female birds exhibited in the arena, and across different song path length ratios ( [Extended Data Fig. 6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#F10)- [7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#F11)). This indicates a remarkably strong and consistent preference by female birds to a previously uncharacterized aspect of male song behavior. Finding that imitated songs have longer path lengths in latent feature space than improvised songs and that female birds strongly prefer songs with longer path lengths shows that this can function as a novel metric used in examining mate selection and song fitness.

### Imitating Song Path Lengths

If path length functions as an indicator of male quality, then we might predict that learning longer path length songs incurs a greater cost during the developmental song learning process [38](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#R38)- [41](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#R41). In other words, it should be more difficult to imitate longer path length songs. To examine this, we used 8 adult birds with imitated songs from our cohort of birds illustrated in [Fig. 2c](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#F2) and established them as breeders. We then tracked the normal song imitation of 2 or 3 of the male offspring from each of these birds ( [Fig. 4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#F4)).

### Figure 4. Imitating the path length of longer path length songs is difficult.

![Figure 4.](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/0e83/11410162/093bcaad8923/nihms-2018754-f0004.jpg)

[Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/figure/F4/)

(a) Spectrograms of three pupil-tutor pairs illustrating good song imitation by pupils (bottom row) of their adult tutor song (top row). The SAP acoustic similarity score is below the spectrograms and the path length of each song is given in the top right of each spectrogram. (b) SAP acoustic similarity scores are not correlated with path length of tutor song (linear regression p = 0.45, R2 = −0.5, n = 17 pupil birds). (c) Change in pupil song path length compared to tutor path length. Zero on Y-axis indicate equal path lengths between pupil and tutor. Pupils produce shorter path length songs when tutored by birds with long path length (linear regression p = 0.01, R2 = 0.62, n = 17 pupil birds).

We first asked if the path length of a tutor’s song had any effect on juvenile birds’ ability to imitate the acoustic features of song. We measured the percent similarity of tutor and pupil song using Song Analysis Pro (SAP), a standard software package calculating an integrated measure of pitch, frequency modulation, amplitude modulation, Wiener entropy, and goodness of pitch to measure acoustic similarity[42](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#R42). We found that all pupils learned reasonably well when analyzed with SAP and there was no correlation between adult similarity scores and the path length of the tutor’s song ( [Fig. 4b](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#F4)).

We next asked how well pupils matched the path length of their tutor’s song by comparing the change in path length of each pupils’ adult song to the path length of their tutor. For this we made a new UMAP plot projecting DAN-segmented syllable spectrograms from the adult songs of the 17 pupils and their 8 tutors. We found a significant positive correlation between the original path lengths of the 8 tutors and their path lengths in the new embedding space that included their offspring ( _p_ = 0.03, R2 = 0.54), again underscoring the stability of song path length representations across different UMAP embeddings ( [Extended Data Fig. 8](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#F12)).

When comparing the change in path length of the pupil song with the tutor-song, we found a significant negative correlation ( [Fig. 4c](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#F4)). Juvenile birds tutored by birds with short path length songs were able to match or exceed the path length of their tutor while birds tutored by birds with long path length songs struggled to match those path lengths by adulthood. Together, these findings indicate that it is challenging for male zebra finches to learn to sing a song that females prefer and that standard acoustic similarity measures are insensitive to this critical aspect of song.

### Distinctiveness of Song Syllables

Having demonstrated that the path length of zebra finch song may function as an indicator of male quality and that this is not captured by a well-established acoustic measure of song similarity, we looked back at our data to examine how path length might potentially reflect movement through a measurable acoustic space. Using multiple linear regression across 119,963 song syllables evenly subsampled from the 49 birds in [Fig. 2c](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#F2), we examined if individual spectral or temporal parameters typically used in quantification of vocalizations could account for the spatial distribution of syllable clusters in UMAP space [Extended Data Table 2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#T2), [Extended Data Fig. 9a](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#F13)). We found that almost all acoustic features are significantly correlated with UMAP dimensions and that the mean pitch of syllables has the strongest correlation ( _p_ < 0.0001). However, these correlations individually accounted for only a small proportion of variance in UMAP coordinates (R2 ≤ 0.10), suggesting that this reflects a complex multi-dimensional acoustic space.

To further examine this issue, we first used correlation analysis between acoustic parameters, but this failed to identify strong clusters of correlated features, beyond those readily expected based on measurement statistics ( [Extended Data Fig. 9b](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#F13)). Next, we used a generalized linear model (GLM) to try and directly predict pairwise syllable distance in UMAP space from differences in acoustic parameters (2,280 syllables evenly sample from 49 birds in [Fig. 2c](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#F2), see [Methods](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#S7)). Our GLM identified pairwise changes in Wiener entropy, a measure of noisiness, and frequency modulation as the two strongest single predictors, and change in mean pitch of the syllable, in combination with either syllable duration, peak frequency, goodness of pitch, or amplitude as significant positive or negative predictors of pairwise distance between syllables in UMAP space ( _p_ \> 0.001, [Extended Data Table 3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#T3)). These findings suggest that path length captures movement across a multi-dimensional acoustic space that likely reflects the spectrotemporal distinctiveness of song syllables.

## DISCUSSION

Oscine songbirds comprise nearly half of all extant species of birds, and they exhibit remarkable vocal learning abilities, which are thought to have evolved through female sexual selection. Zebra finches are arguably the best studied species of songbird. Yet, it has remained unclear how their single song repertoire functions as an honest indicator of fitness used by female birds in mate selection. We show that the spread of song in latent space, defined by the minimum path length connecting song syllables, is a robust and reliable measure of zebra finch song. Imitated songs have longer path lengths than improvised songs. It is challenging for males to match the long path length songs of a tutor, suggesting that this provides a sensitive measure that may veridically reflect developmental history and male quality. Lastly, we show that female birds are exquisitely sensitive to path length, preferring longer path length songs to shorter path length songs. Given these findings, we hypothesize that this _gestalt_ measure of song syllable diversity captures some key aspect of what female birds are using to evaluate fitness and select between partners.

Our findings therefore help clarify how simple vocal repertoires may have evolved in songbirds. Male songbirds initially learn their song(s) as juveniles. The complexity of adult song, typically measured as repertoire size or song bout duration, is hypothesized to provide an honest indicator of developmental conditions and how well individuals responded to environmental or nutritional stressors[39](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#R39), [40](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#R40) – ultimately reflecting the genetic and/or cognitive fitness of individual animals. This idea, termed the developmental stress hypothesis, has gained strong support from studies in a range of birds that produce multiple song types and syntactically complex songs. However, support for this idea has been mixed in zebra finches (reviewed by [38](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#R38)). Although we still know little about how the explicit acoustic structure of song influences female preference, our data indicates that female zebra finches attend to the multi-dimensional spectrotemporal distinctiveness of song syllables, captured here as the spread of song in latent space. This result suggests that sexual selection is not only associated with the evolution of large and complex vocal repertoires but also with the evolution of what we perceive, perhaps naively, as a simple song.

The key insights of this research emerged by using latent representations of imitated and improvised song to develop a parsimonious hypothesis for what female birds may or may not prefer. Our measure of song has eluded previous research because it is not reflected through measures of manually selected acoustic features, and thus, also evades standard measures of song similarity. By using a data-driven approach and validating through female song preferences, we have identified a sensitive metric that can be used to examine if a male has learned a song that will help him attract at mate.

## METHODS

### Animals

Experiments described in this study were conducted using adult male and female zebra finches (>100 days post hatch (dph)). We raised juvenile male zebra finches either in isolation from an adult song model (improvised group), or with un-restricted access to an adult song model (imitated group). For the improvised group fathers were removed from these breeding cages prior to 12 days post hatching and birds were raised with their mother and siblings (brothers and sisters) in a room that only had cages housing other birds in the improvised group. Juvenile males in the improvised group were weaned between 45-60 days post hatching and each male bird was raised in a separate cage within a larger acoustic chamber that also housed cages with other individual young birds in the improvised group. Although these birds were able to hear one another, we blocked visual access between cages, which is sufficient to block horizontal and vertical transmission of song. Our tutored birds were raised in our main colony that contains ~55 breeding pairs. These young birds have continual access to the father’s song and can hear birds in adjacent cages. They were similarly weaned from the breeding cage between 45-60 days post hatching. All but one female bird used in this study had un-restricted access to an adult song model. This female bird also preferred long path length song. All procedures were performed in accordance with protocols approved by Animal Care and Use Committee at UT Southwestern Medical Center.

To obtain song recordings, male zebra finches of at least 100 dph were individually housed in sound attenuating chambers for a period of up to 2 weeks. They were continuously recorded using Sound Analysis Pro 2011(SAP)[43](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#R43). All zebra finches were placed on a 14/10 day/night cycle and provided with ad libitum access to food, water, and grit.

To habituate the zebra finches to the experimental conditions used in our place preference tests, the female zebra finches were individually housed in sound-attenuated chambers with a 14/10 day/night cycle and provided with ad libitum access to food, water, and grit for at least 72 hours before the start of the place preference experiments.

### Deep Avian Network

#### Song Recording and Segmentation

Adult male zebra finches of at least 100 dph were individually recorded in sound attenuating chambers for a period of up to 2 weeks using SAP[43](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#R43). Amplitude variance was calculated on recorded raw SAP audio files. We then apply a gaussian filter to the resulting amplitude variance. To identify onsets and offsets of sound and silence, we direct the identification of local maxima and local minima in the lower range of the variance. Once the range was defined, we calculated the first derivative of the amplitude variance to identify periods of transition from silence to sound and sound to silence. These onset and offset times were used to segment the audio file into audio segments.

#### Feature Based Clustering of Training Data

Duration, root mean square of amplitude, goodness of pitch, mean frequency, entropy, zero crossing rate, and power spectrum density are calculated for all segmented audio files. Acoustic features, with the exception of duration, are then scaled using the Scikit-learn Robustscaler function[44](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#R44). The coefficients of spectrograms composed of 28 time components and 28 frequency ranging from 0 to 255. All inputs are then individually reduced to 2 dimensions using UMAP. These reduced features from both the acoustic analysis and spectrogram coefficients are then combined using the UMAP union operation. This produces a comprehensive projection composed of each individual feature's reduction. The resulting embeddings are then clustered together through hierarchical clustering using the fcluster function to form training data for the convolutional neural network.

#### Siamese Convolutional Neural Network

The Siamese Convolutional Neural Network (sCNN) neural network is created and trained within Deep Avian Network using TensorFlow. For the sCNN, convolutional layers are used to detect diverse patterns and features within the input data. The integration of batch normalization contributes to stabilizing and expediting the training process by standardizing the activations of the preceding layer. Dropout layers serve as a preventive measure against overfitting by introducing regularization—randomly discarding a fraction of neuron outputs during training. Max pooling layers are used for down-sampling and diminishing spatial dimensions. Fully connected layers serve as the engine to acquire feature representations. Finally, the use of dense layers facilitates the fusion of information gathered from prior layers, culminating in the generation of an output. Details of the sCNN are in [Extended Data Table 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#T1).

#### Precision and Recall Scores

Precision is the proportion of positively predicted labels over the false positives and true positives (TP/(FP+TP). It is a measure of how many identified elements are relevant. Recall is the proportion of positively predicted labels over the false negatives and true positives. It is a measure of how many identified elements are retrieved. For segmentation precision scores and recall scores reflect, the segments detected compared against manually identified segments. Machine and deep learning scores reflect the correct assignments to each cluster compared to manually identified assignments of clusters.

### Female Preference Testing

To assess female song preferences, we used a large free flight T-maze. The T-maze is comprised of three arms, each containing a perch. The home arm is 22x15x15 inches, and each playback arm is 29x15x15 inches. The playback arms are separated by an opaque divider. Speakers rest atop the end of each playback arm. Equal amounts of seed and fruit were placed in each playback arm at the beginning of each day to encourage exploration of the T-maze.

Adult females of at least 100 dph were individually housed in sound attenuating chambers. During experimental days, access to food was limited for a period of 3 hours in the morning. The female zebra finches were then placed inside the T-maze with access to 2 grams of a combination of seed, fruit, or vegetables. During a period of habituation, zebra finch movement was tracked using video recordings. Birds that sufficiently explored all three arms of the T-maze were next screened to test whether they would exhibit changes in place preferences in response to audio playbacks of a wildtype song and white noise (WN; [Extended Data Fig. 10](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#F14)). For this screening, baseline place preference was determined over a period of 5 minutes. Wildtype song was played in the non-preferred arm and white noise was played in the preferred arm. Wildtype song and WN play back were alternated, cycling every 15 seconds for a period of 5 minutes. Birds were again tracked for 5 minutes to examine if they exhibited place preference after cessation of audio playback. Birds that exhibited place preferences for wildtype song during audio playback were then given a rest period of at least 1 hour before being tested for their place preference for long vs short path length songs.

Synthetic long and short path length songs were created by sampling from song syllables produced from the 31 birds with imitated songs in the UMAP space illustrated in [Fig. 2c](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#F2). We created 3 pairs of synthetic songs with each pair differing in their path length distance (see [Extended Data Fig. 5d](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#F9)). All synthetic songs contained 5 syllables with 25 ms gaps between the syllables. Syllables used to create song were selected to have similar acoustic features, including similar entropy, frequency modulation, goodness of pitch, amplitude modulation, and duration, and several syllables were interchangeably used in long and short path synthetic songs (see [Fig. S5](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#SD7)); thus, helping to isolate path length as the distinguishing feature between songs. Each female was presented with a single pair of synthetic song. Two consecutive motifs, without introductory notes, were played at 15 second intervals during the 5-minute song playback period. In initial experiments, the long path length song was played in the non-preferred arm and the short path length song was played in the preferred arm, resulting in birds switching their place preference during song playback. Subsequently we found that baseline arm preference was inconsequential and playing the long path song in the preferred also resulted in female birds spending even more time in the preferred arm.

Bird position in the maze was tracked and quantified using DeepLab Cut[45](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#R45) and independently confirmed by blinded human scorers measuring the time spent in each arm.

### Song Analysis

#### UMAP and Clustering

We embedded spectrograms of downscaled individual segmented syllables using Euclidean UMAP. After we individually scaled and zero padded spectrograms of syllables for uniformity, we downscaled into 28 frequency components and 28 time components. The resulting dataset was then further reduced to 2 dimensions using UMAP calculated with 15 nearest neighbors and a minimum distance of 0.1. We verified syllable cluster identity by cross checking hand-labeled assignments. Parametric UMAP was computed with differing global weights. To measure clustering of the embedded data, we applied HDBSCAN[46](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#R46) with default parameters and K-means from Scikit-learn[2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#R2), set to the number of hand-identified clusters, and additionally used Silhouette scores to measure how well the clustering methods performed.

To measure the path length within an individual’s song, we first calculated the geometric mean of all syllables within a cluster. We then calculated the Euclidean distance between the geometric means of each cluster within a bird’s song, measuring all permutations of syllable order for each bird’s song. Minimum distance was identified as the smallest trajectory between syllable clusters belonging to one individual. We also obtained similar results when calculating Manhattan distance or Chebyshev distance.

#### Analysis of Acoustic Features in UMAP Embeddings

To examine correlations between syllable embeddings in UMAP feature space and acoustic parameters we used multiple linear regression and generalized linear models (GLMs). We extracted all song syllables produced in 100 randomly selected song bouts from each of the 49 birds plotted in [Figure 2c](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#F2) using SAP[43](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#R43). This resulted in 119,963 song syllables, approximately one quarter of the dataset used in the UMAP plotted in [Fig. 2c](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#F2). We measured 31 acoustic parameters from each of these syllables using SAP (see [Extended Data Table 2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#T2) for list of acoustic parameters). We next generated spectrograms for each syllable and embedded them using Euclidean UMAP, as described above. We then used multiple linear regression to examine correlations between each acoustic parameter and the positions of song syllable spectrograms plotted in UMAP1 and UMAP2 latent feature space. To further analyze correlations, we examined Pearson correlation coefficients between UMAP1 and UMAP2 as well as between different acoustic metrics. The correlations coefficients (r) are reported in the correlation matrix in [Fig. S4b](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#SD7).

We used a GLM to try and predict distance in UMAP space from differences in acoustic parameters. For this we chose a subset of syllables (10 syllables) from every syllable ‘type’ produced by the 49 birds plotted in [figure 2c](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#F2) (228 syllables ‘types’ x 10 versions of each syllable = 2,280 syllables). We then calculated the UMAP coordinates and eight core acoustic features for each syllable (duration, mean pitch, mean peak frequency, mean frequency modulation, mean entropy, mean goodness of pitch, mean amplitude modulation, and mean amplitude). We computed the pair-wise UMAP distance between every pair of syllables and their pair-wise feature space difference for each acoustic parameter. We used a GLM to predict UMAP distance between syllable pairs from a combination of acoustic feature distances between the same pairs. To build the GLM we first ran GLMs including the eight acoustic features and a single interaction variable that included two or more of the acoustic features. For variable selection we examined the coefficients for all possible combinations of the acoustic features (238 iterations) while holding the eight single acoustic features constant across models. Our final model, ( [Extended Data Table 3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11410162/#T3)), included the pair-wise feature space difference for each of eight acoustic parameters and all interactions with coefficients larger than +/− 0.10, (four sets of interactions: duration\*mean pitch, mean amplitude\*mean pitch, mean pitch\*mean goodness of pitch, mean pitch\*mean peak frequency).

#### Statistical Analysis:

All collected data were tested for normality using the D’agostino Pearson omnibus test. Based on the results, appropriate parametric or non-parametric tests were chosen. Unpaired or paired t-tests were conducted as indicated when appropriate. For comparisons spanning over more than two conditions, a one-way ANOVA was employed. Significance levels were denoted as follows: \*P < 0.05, \*\*P < 0.01, \*\*\*P < 0.001. Detailed statistical information for all experiments is provided in the corresponding figure legends.

### Extended Data Table 2. Linear regression analysis of SAP features across UMAP latent space.

### Extended Data Table 3. Results of Generalized Linear Model.

Results of GLM predicting pairwise syllable distance in UMAP space from differences in acoustic parameters (2,280 syllables evenly sample from 49 birds). Our GLM identified pairwise changes in (Wiener) entropy, and frequency modulation as the two strongest single predictors, and change in mean pitch of the syllable, in combination with either syllable duration, peak frequency, goodness of pitch, or amplitude as significant positive or negative predictors of pairwise distance between syllables in UMAP space.

## Editors

- Reviewing Editor

Yoko Yazaki-Sugiyama

Okinawa Institute of Science and Technology Graduate University, Onna-son, Japan

- Senior Editor

Claude Desplan

New York University, New York, United States of America


**Reviewer #3 (Public review):**

Summary:

Davenport et al have investigated how a masculinizing dose of estrogen changes the transcriptomes of several key song nuclei song and adjacent brain areas in juvenile zebra finches of both sexes. Only male zebra finches sing, learn song, and normally have a fully developed song control circuitry, so the study was aimed at further understanding how genetic and hormonal factors contribute to the dimorphism in song behavior and related brain circuitry in this species. Using WGCNA and follow-up correlations to re-analyze published transcriptome datasets, the authors provide evidence that the main variance of several identified gene co-expression modules significantly correlates with one or some of the factors examined, including sex, estrogen treatment, regional neuroanatomy, chromosomal placement, or vocal learning, noting that the latter is largely based on inference due to expression in song control nuclei.

Strengths:

Among the main strengths are the thorough gene co-expression module and correlation analyses, and the inclusion of both song nuclei and adjacent areas, the latter serving as sort of controls for areas that are not dimorphic and likely broadly present in birds in general. In situ hybridization data discussed in a previous publication (Choe et al., Hormones and Behavior, 2021) provides some support for the neuroanatomical specializations of gene expression. It is also significant that the transcriptome re-analysis was performed with an improved genome assembly that also includes the sex chromosomes, thus expanding the Z/W chromosome gene analyses in Friedrich et al, Cell Reports, 2022. The most relevant finding is arguably the identification of some modules where gene expression variation within song nuclei correlates with hormonal effects and/or gene location on sex chromosomes, which are present at different dosages between sexes. Sex differences in gene expression in areas that are not song nuclei may also bring insights into functions other than song behavior or vocal learning. The study also shows how a published RNA-seq dataset can be reanalyzed in novel and informative ways.

Weaknesses:

The validation of the inferred direction of regulation in the identified co-expression modules is limited to the in situ data mentioned above. Further evidence that representative genes in the main modules differ in expression when comparing sexes or E2- vs VEH-treated tissues using independent samples and/or methods would provide further validation and enhance rigor. Most importantly, E2 is known to exert various actions on brain physiology and neuronal function. Because there was no manipulation of candidate genes, nor assessment/manipulation of vocal behavior or vocal learning, an involvement of the identified candidate genes in setting up the sexual dimorphism of the song system or song behavior was not directly tested in this study. For the latter reason, the implication of the Title (..."gene expression associated with vocal learning...") is not well supported. While novel insights were gained into brain expression of Z chromosome genes, it cannot be excluded that the higher male expression of some Z genes may not affect brain cell function and thus may not require active compensation (as discussed for nucleus RA in Friedrich et al, Cell Reports, 2022).

The following is the authors’ response to the original reviews.

> **eLife Assessment**
>
> This study is useful as it provides further analysis of previously published data to address which specific genes are part of the masculinizing actions of E2 on female zebra finches, and where these key genes are expressed in the brain. However the data supporting the conclusion of masculinizing the song system are incomplete as the current manuscript is a re-analysis of differential gene expression modulated by E2 treatment between male/female zebra finches without manipulation of gene expression. The conclusions (and title) regarding song learning are also incompletely supported with no gene manipulation or song analysis. Importantly, the use of WGCNA for a question of sex-chromosome expression in species without dosage compensation is considered inadequate. As the experimental design did not include groups to directly test for song learning, and there was also no analysis of song performance, these data were also considered inadequate in that regard.

We are sorry the editor felt the manuscript so incomplete and inadequate. Though the tone of this assessment seems more severe than the below reviewer comments, we are also happy to see that the editor has considered our paper further for a revised publication, based on the reviewer’s comments. We address the editor’s comments as follows:

While we agree that manipulation of some of the genes we discovered, whose expression levels are E2-sensitive in the song system, would take the study further in validating some proposed hypothesis in the discussion of the paper, we don’t think the outcome of gene manipulations would change the major conclusions from the results of the paper. In this study we performed estrogen hormone manipulations, with causal consequences on gene expression in song nuclei and associated song behavior. In a way this is analogous to gene manipulations, but manipulating directly the action of estrogen. The categories of genes impacted, and the differences among the sex chromosomes wouldn’t change.

For the comment on WGCNA being inadequate for addressing questions on sex chromosome expression in species without dosage compensation, we think the evidence in our data does not bear that out. One main result of this paper is the separation of Z chromosome transcripts whose expression is most strongly regulated by chromosomal dosage (WGCNA module E) across regions from those subject to additional sources of regulation in song nuclei (other modules). It seems to us that rather than being confounded by the lack of dosage compensation, WGCNA allowed us to better resolve the effects of dosage on different genes within the sex chromosomes. We have added a new figure more directly examining sex chromosome transcript abundance within different modules. Briefly, we found that module E assigned Z chromosome genes exhibited almost exactly the male-biased expression ratio expected from no dosage compensation while the Z chromosome genes in song nuclei assigned to other modules were expressed below the dosage predicted value, consistent with module E containing those genes whose expression are most strongly regulated by dose across all brain regions sampled.

At its core, WGCNA finds sets of correlated genes. The biological reality of the zebra finch transcriptome is that Z chromosome expression is largely anti-correlated with W chromosome due to dosage. However, this dosage effect is not felt equally by all genes and WGCNA provides an unbiased computational framework which can be used to separate dose from other potential sources of gene regulation. This is why roughly ⅓ of Z chromosome genes are not assigned to module E; for example the growth hormone receptor is assigned to module G based on its correlation with genes upregulated within HVC.

> “As the experimental design did not include groups to directly test for song learning, and there was also no analysis of song performance, these data were also considered inadequate in that regard.”

Concerning the comment on no analysis on song performance in the paper, all such analyses were conducted on our previous study on the same animals (Choe et al. 2021, Hormones & Behavior). The birds considered here were sacrificed at PHD30, prior to the onset of learned song behavior. However, females treated with E2 the same at the same time and allowed to mature into adulthood, went onto to develop rudimentary song. Further, induction of rudimentary song learning in females following E2 treatment has been well established since the early ‘80s. We have added the following text toward the end of the intro to make this more clear:

“While the birds for this study were sacrificed prior to the developmental presentation of song behavior, we have previously shown that female finches treated in exactly the say way with E2 go on to produce rudimentary imitative songs as adults (Choe et al 2021), consistent with the known induction of vocal learning in females by E2 (REF).”

> **Reviewer #2 (Recommendations For The Authors):**

> Since WGCNA methods will inherently draw together sex-chromosome genes into the same module in systems without dosage compensation, I suggest the authors rerun the WGCNA using only female samples and only male samples. Then identify the composition of modules that differ between E2 and vehicle-treated females and compare these genes to males. Then from male WGCNA identify the composition of modules that differ between E2 and vehicle-treated males and compare to female modules.

We thank the reviewer for their suggestions. However, we believe it is not as strong as the approach we used, which is grouping data from both sexes in the WGCNA analyses in a study that is looking for sex differences. The reviewer's proposed approach amounts to computing modules twice (once per sex), determining song system specialized modules and E2 responsive modules in both settings, then intersecting the two sets to find corresponding modules, all done to prevent the non-dose compensated sex chromosome genes from being drawn into the same module.

While WGCNA does group the majority of sex chromosome genes into module E, it does not categorize them all this way (Fig 3). The module classification instead differentiates those sex chromosome genes whose expression are most explained by chromosome dosage / sex across regions (modE) from those whose expression is controlled by other sources of regulation; for an example of the latter, the growth hormone receptor (GHR) is one of several Z chromosome genes classified into modG as its expression better correlates with the genes specialized to HVC than it does with the majority of dosage-dependent Z chromosome genes found in modE. Further, to remove biological sex as a variable in a WGCNA analysis that is focused on sex differences seems counterintuitive.

Instead, to quantitatively address the reviewer’s concern, we conducted additional analyses, that led to an added new figure, associated text, and tables, that better describes sex/chromosome dosage effects on the abundance (FPKM) and expression ratios of sex chromosome transcripts by module irrespective of brain region (Fig. 5). We find that the Z chromosome genes in modE were expressed at the expected chromosome dosage in the non-vocal surrounding regions (65.06% observed vs 66.6% expected) while in other modules, other Z chromosome genes were expressed at intermediate levels between equal expression and the expected chromosomal dosage. For example, the Z chromosome content of modules D and H exhibited near equal expression between sexes. Within the song system, Z chromosome gene content of modG was highly expressed in males beyond what is expected from chromosome dosage, consistent with modG’s male-specific upregulation in song nuclei relative to surrounds in the absence of E2. These results better demonstrate that in our WGCNA on the combined dataset we are able to separate those Z chromosome genes whose expression is predominantly dosage controlled from those subject to additional regulation such as song system specialization.

Fig. S5 was the same type of analyses in Fig. 3 but with an older zebra finch genome assembly, where we had not included the panel a for enrichments with genes convergent in expression between songbird song regions and humans speech brain regions. However, we see that Fig. S5 was not adding any new important information to the paper, so we removed it.

For the chromosome analyses in Fig. 3b, we provide both the total raw number of module assigned genes broken down by chromosome (The black bar plots on the right) as well as a statistical fold-enrichment value of modules per chromosome. Given the number of genes per chromosome and genes per module in our data, we computed the fold-enrichment for each intersection (observed intersection size / expected intersection size). To test for the significance of these enrichments, we bootstrapped FDR corrected p values for the enrichment of each chromosome-module pairing by randomizing the mapping of genes to modules to construct a null distribution of fold enrichments for each intersection. Our intent was not to describe the size of the chromosomes themselves, information readily available elsewhere, but to show the disproportionate chromosomal origins of the gene sets considered by this study. Performing this enrichment test using all annotated genes per chromosome would artificially increase enrichment values and make the analysis less conservative by confounding the results with the inherent enrichment for “brain function” in the assigned genes relative to all genes.

We interpret this result as the Z chromosome genes being among the earliest genes differentially expressed between the sexes, not that their differential expression or role ever subsequently decreased. We have reworked this portion of the discussion to make our point more clear:

“This model of sex chromosome influenced song system development is consistent with recent observations comparing male and female zebra finch transcriptomes from RA at young juvenile (PHD20) and young adult (PHD50) ages in un-manipulated birds (Friedrich et al. 2022)57. While that study proposes that the role of the sex chromosome in maintaining transcriptomic sex differences diminishes across development, as the proportion of specialized genes that originate on the sex chromosomes diminishes, this effect was driven by large increases in differentially expressed autosomal genes rather than by any reduction in sex chromosome dimorphism; the percentage of differentially expressed Z chromosome genes increased from PHD20 (28%) to PHD50 (39%) (Friedrich et al). This leads us to conclude that sexually dimorphic Z chromosome expression at juvenile ages precedes the sexually dimorphic expression of the autosomes seen in adults. This is consistent with our hypothesis that sufficient expression of select Z chromosome gene products (GHR, etc..) is necessary for subsequent autosomal song system specializations (modG).”

Further, when we write ”When examining the module G HVC specialization induced by E2-treatment in female HVC, we surprisingly found that the most specialized genes were disproportionately from the Z chromosome” we are referring to the upregulation of module G by E2 in female HVC, not the sex difference described in RA by Friedrich et al. which only utilized un-treated RA samples and thus is more likely related to our observations of module E.

> The term "sexual dimorphism" has been more traditionally used for sex differences that are very marked, like features that are highly regressed or absent in one sex, most often in females. Quantitative differences in gene expression, including dosage differences like those related to module E, are more appropriately described as sex differences rather than dimorphisms. That usage would be more consistent with most of the literature, and thus preferable.

We did a google search for common definitions, and found more the opposite. Sexual dimorphism being used more often as differences of degree (with the zebra finch example as one of the top hits), and sex differences being used often as more absolute differences (like presence vs absence of the Y chromosome). Further, as in the reviewer’s first sentence, the definition of sexual dimorphism is a sex difference. That is, the two phrases can be interchangeable. Thus, we prefer to keep sexual dimorphism.

> Several references are incomplete or seem truncated, like 9 and 10.

Fixed

> Table S2: Please examine and take into account the W gene curation presented in Table S3 of Friedrich et al., 2022.

We have added additional supplementals (supplemetal\_w\_chrom\_express.csv and supplemetal\_z\_chrom\_express.csv) of the data provided in new Fig 5 incorporating the curation information from Table S3 from Friedrich et al.

We have added an additional Supplemental Table supplemental\_gene\_module\_assignment.csv with this information.

## Summary

Learning by imitation is the foundation for verbal and musical expression, but its underlying neural basis remains obscure. A juvenile male zebra finch imitates the multisyllabic song of an adult tutor in a process that depends on a song-specialized cortico-basal ganglia circuit, affording a powerful system to identify the synaptic substrates of imitative motor learning. Plasticity at a particular set of cortico-basal ganglia synapses is hypothesized to drive rapid learning-related changes in song before these changes are subsequently consolidated in downstream circuits. Nevertheless, this hypothesis is untested and the synaptic locus where learning initially occurs is unknown. By combining a computational framework to quantify song learning with synapse-specific optogenetic and chemogenetic manipulations within and directly downstream of the cortico-basal ganglia circuit, we identified the specific cortico-basal ganglia synapses that drive the acquisition and expression of rapid vocal changes during juvenile song learning and characterized the hours-long timescale over which these changes consolidate. Furthermore, transiently augmenting postsynaptic activity in the basal ganglia briefly accelerates learning rates and persistently alters song, demonstrating a direct link between basal ganglia activity and rapid learning. These results localize the specific cortico-basal ganglia synapses that enable a juvenile songbird to learn to sing and reveal the circuit logic and behavioral timescales of this imitative learning paradigm.

## Introduction

A juvenile male zebra finch memorizes the multisyllabic song motif of an adult male tutor and then engages in a prolonged (∼1 month) period of hearing-dependent vocal practice, known as sensorimotor learning, to imitate this memorized model[1](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#ref-1)– [5](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#ref-5) ( [Fig. 1a](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#F1)). A song-specialized cortico-basal ganglia pathway is essential to this spontaneous form of sensorimotor learning[6](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#ref-6)– [9](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#ref-9), affording the potential to identify the neural substrates that enable song imitation ( [Fig. 1b](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#F1)). An influential model is that two distinct cortical inputs into a song-specialized region of the basal ganglia (Area X, referred to here as the sBG) convey separate signals related to song timing and variability, the coincident activity of which generates a synaptic eligibility trace in the spiny neurons that make up >95% of sBG neurons[10](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#ref-10)– [15](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#ref-15). In this model[15](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#ref-15), the initial acquisition and expression of sensorimotor learning depend on a dopamine-mediated reinforcement signal acting on the eligibility trace to selectively strengthen the time-encoding cortico-sBG spiny neuron synapses whose singing-related activity corresponds with song variants more like the tutor ( [Fig. 1c](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#F1)). This synaptic change alters sBG song premotor activity to bias the juvenile’s song towards the tutor model, an effect that is then consolidated over a longer timescale in motor regions downstream of the cortico-sBG pathway[16](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#ref-16), [17](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#ref-17).

Although this model[15](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#ref-15) is the dominant theoretical description of song learning, it remains largely untested. Whether sBG premotor activity actually drives progressive changes in song during juvenile learning remains unknown. Additionally, which synapses change within the sBG to drive the initial expression of song learning and the timescales over which these rapid changes undergo consolidation is unclear. However, one prediction of this model is that transiently suppressing sBG activity, and specifically the time-encoding cortico-sBG synapses, during singing will prevent the expression of recently learned changes in song but not changes that have already been consolidated. A second prediction of this model is that the initial acquisition of learning is localized to the sBG and requires activity in both the time- and variability-encoding cortico-sBG inputs.

To directly test these predictions, we combined computational methods to quantify rendition-to-rendition vocal changes during juvenile song learning[18](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#ref-18), [19](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#ref-19) with closed-loop optogenetic suppression or chemogenetic manipulations of specific pre- and postsynaptic components of the cortico-sBG circuit, as well as in downstream cortical premotor-motor circuitry. This approach enabled us to identify and distinguish synaptic loci crucial to the initial acquisition and expression of sensorimotor learning, quantify the timescale over which these neural and vocal changes undergo consolidation, and explore how sBG activity influences the quality and rate of learning.

### sBG activity is necessary to express recently learned changes to song

To establish a causal link between sBG activity and the expression of song learning, we quantified how transiently suppressing sBG activity affected rapid, learning-related changes in juvenile song. We expressed the inhibitory opsin Archaerhodopsin[20](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#ref-20) (ArchT) or a control fluorophore (tdTomato) in the sBG of normally tutored juvenile male zebra finches using viral vectors (injected 20 - 35 days post hatch (dph), n = 3 birds using AAV2/1-CAG-ArchT-eGFP for pan-neuronal expression; n = 3 birds using AAV2/1-CaMKIIa-ArchT-eGFP for spiny neuron specific expression[21](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#ref-21); n = 4 birds using AAV2/1-CAG-tdTomato as a control; each bird was housed in their home cage from hatching with their mother, father (an adult male tutor), and siblings). Three weeks later (41 - 56 dph), we verified that sBG action potential activity was suppressed by laser administration in ArchT- but not tdTomato-expressing birds and then implanted tapered optical fibers bilaterally in the sBG ( [Fig. 1d](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#F1); [Extended Data Fig. 1a](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#F5)). Following implantation, birds were isolated from their tutors and individually housed in sound attenuating chambers, tethered to optical fibers, and their songs were recorded continuously until ∼90 dph, when sensorimotor learning typically ends. On alternating weekdays, we detected the introductory notes[22](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#ref-22) that occur at the beginning of song motifs and administered 2 s of laser to the sBG on a random 10% of song renditions, a period sufficient to encompass one to several song motifs ( [Fig 1e](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#F1)).

To quantify the juvenile song learning trajectory, we segmented the entire corpus of recorded song motifs into their component syllables (3.6 million syllable renditions from 10 birds). We then used a Variational Autoencoder (VAE) to compress high dimensional (128 x 128 pixels, ∼16K) spectrographic data into 32 latent dimensions that preserved information sufficient to accurately reconstruct the original spectrograms[18](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#ref-18) ( [Fig. 1f](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#F1)).

Two-dimensional UMAP projections of these latent embeddings revealed that individual syllables were well-clustered and distinct from one another ( [Fig. 1g](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#F1), left). Qualitatively, labeling syllable renditions by their age of production revealed that syllable representations in this latent space occupied different locations as a function of the age of production, corresponding to maturation of the underlying spectrogram ( [Fig. 1g](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#F1), right). To quantify this relationship on a rendition-by-rendition basis, we trained an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) to predict the most likely age of production for each syllable rendition based on its location in this latent space[19](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#ref-19) ( [Fig. 1h](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#F1)). This VAE-ANN predicted age metric is hereafter referred to as “syllable maturity” or simply “maturity”.

The VAE-ANN method allowed us to assess how transiently suppressing sBG activity during singing affected syllable maturity. To quantify this, we built linear mixed effects models to predict how laser affected syllable maturity in both ArchT and tdTomato control birds, while also controlling for circadian and developmental contributions to maturity (see methods). Comparing laser-on to laser-off renditions in ArchT birds and in tdTomato fluorophore controls revealed that syllables were less mature when sBG activity was suppressed ( [Fig. 1i,j](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#F1); ArchT: -2.05 ± 0.60 hours (Mean ± SEM), n = 26 ArchT syllables). This regressive effect of sBG suppression on syllable maturity occurred only on targeted syllables and was significantly different from laser shuffled data ( [Extended Data Fig. 1b,c](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#F5)). Finally, syllable maturity was significantly reduced in birds that expressed ArchT either pan-neuronally or only in spiny neurons in the sBG ( [Extended Data Fig. 1d](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#F5)). Thus, singing-related activity specifically in sBG spiny neurons is necessary for the expression of recently learned vocal changes during sensorimotor learning.

Motor learning studies in a variety of species, including zebra finches, indicate that recently learned skills are consolidated during sleep[23](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#ref-23)– [25](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#ref-25). If sleep helps consolidate recently learned song changes downstream of the sBG, then the maturity of renditions produced in the morning just after sleep should be less affected by suppressing sBG activity. However, syllable maturity regressed to a similar extent regardless of when during the day sBG activity was suppressed ( [Extended Data Fig. 1e](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#F5)). We also examined the long-term consequences of intermittent sBG suppression. Final learning outcomes of syllables shared by the pupil and tutor were unaffected by this history of relatively infrequent (i.e. ∼10% of renditions) sBG suppression, underscoring that the protocol used here was able to successfully isolate the sBG’s role in expressing recently learned vocal changes ( [Extended Data Fig. 1f](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#F5)). Notably, the regressive effects on syllable maturity caused by suppressing sBG activity declined steadily over sensorimotor learning ( [Fig. 1k](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#F1)), consistent with various findings that highlight a greater role for cortico-sBG circuits in juvenile sensorimotor learning than in adult song production[6](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#ref-6), [7](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#ref-7), [9](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#ref-9).

Conceivably, suppressing sBG activity could regress syllable maturity by consistently altering certain acoustic features (e.g., pitch, amplitude) that are reliably associated with more mature syllables. However, individual acoustic features did not reliably relate to maturity measured across syllables and, even within syllables, which features predicted maturity could and did change from day to day ( [Fig. 1l](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#F1), [Extended Data Fig. 2a,b](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#F6); see also[26](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#ref-26)). Moreover, suppressing sBG activity did not reliably affect individual acoustic features across syllables or within the same syllable across days ( [Extended Data Fig. 2c,d](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#F6)). In fact, suppressing sBG activity most strongly affected individual acoustic features _if and when_ those features were aligned with syllable maturity ( [Fig. 1m](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#F1), [Extended Data Fig. 2e,f](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#F6)). And while variability measured across all acoustic features decreased slightly when sBG activity was suppressed, variability decreased marginally more in fluorophore control birds, ( [Extended Data Fig. 1g](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#F5)) which exhibited no regression in syllable maturity ( [Fig. 1j](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#F1)). Lastly, suppressing sBG activity did not affect intrasyllabic variability, rendition-to-rendition variability of syllable maturity, syllable syntax, or intersyllabic gaps ( [Extended Data Fig. 2d,h-j](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#F6)). Therefore, in juvenile finches, singing-related sBG activity selectively modulates acoustic features of syllables that align with daily changes in syllable maturity, rather than simply modulating a fixed set of acoustic features or altering the order or timing of syllables.

### The time-encoding cortico-sBG synapse is necessary to express recent learning

The sBG receives singing-related excitatory input from the cortical nuclei HVC and LMAN, which are hypothesized to convey song timing and variability signals, respectively[15](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#ref-15). To further isolate a synaptic locus necessary for the expression of sensorimotor learning, we used viral vectors to express an inhibitory opsin (Parapinopsin, or PPO, a Gi/o-coupled opsin)[27](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#ref-27) bilaterally in either HVC or LMAN axon terminals in the sBG (GFP was expressed in HVC-sBG terminals in an additional control group, n = 5 birds per group) ( [Fig. 2a](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#F2)). Three weeks later (41 - 56 dph), we verified that laser applied to the sBG reduced spontaneous action potential activity in PPO- but not GFP-expressing birds ( [Fig. 2a](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#F2)). We also verified that optogenetically suppressing HVC terminals using PPO could prevent sBG activity evoked by electrical stimulation in HVC (n = 2 birds, [Extended Data Fig. 3a](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#F7)). Following bilateral implantation of optical fibers targeting the sBG, we used the same approach described previously for ArchT experiments ( [Fig. 1e](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#F1)) to suppress either HVC-sBG or LMAN-sBG terminal activity on a small subset (∼10%) of motifs produced across sensorimotor learning (5.6 million syllable renditions from 15 birds).

Comparing laser-on to laser-off renditions revealed that syllables were less mature when HVC-sBG, but not LMAN-sBG, terminals were suppressed ( [Fig. 2b,c](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#F2) HVC-sBG: -1.52 ± 0.58 hours, Mean ± SEM; n = 25 syllables). This regressive effect of HVC-sBG terminal suppression on maturity manifested only on laser-targeted renditions, was significant in comparison with laser shuffled data, and was not elicited by laser illumination of the sBG in GFP controls ( [Extended Data Fig. 3b,c](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#F7)). Variability measured across all acoustic features was unaffected by suppressing either HVC-sBG or LMAN-sBG terminals ( [Extended Data Fig. 3d](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#F7)). Along with the spiny neuron-specific sBG suppression experiments ( [Extended Data Fig. 1d](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#F5)), these observations indicate that the rapid expression of vocal changes during sensorimotor learning involves HVC axon terminal synapses on sBG spiny neurons[15](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#ref-15).

Prior studies showed that the expression of a simpler form of externally reinforced vocal learning in adult finches (“pitch learning”) depends on activity conveyed by the output of the cortico-sBG circuitry, namely the axonal projections from LMAN to the cortical song premotor nucleus RA[28](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#ref-28). To test whether a similar mechanism underlies the expression of juvenile sensorimotor learning, we explored how syllable maturity was affected by transient optogenetic suppression of LMAN-RA terminals during juvenile learning (n = 4 LMAN-RA PPO and n = 4 LMAN-RA GFP control birds; 2.1 million syllable renditions, [Fig. 2d](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#F2)). Prior to behavioral experiments, we verified that spontaneous action potential activity in RA was suppressed by laser administration in PPO- but not GFP-expressing birds ( [Fig. 2d](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#F2)). Indeed, PPO-mediated suppression in RA was as efficacious as the cortico-sBG PPO manipulations ( [Extended Data Fig. 3e,f](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#F7)). Comparing laser-on to laser-off renditions revealed that syllables were less mature in LMAN-RA PPO but not GFP control birds ( [Fig 2e](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#F2), LMAN-RA PPO: -1.36 ± 0.43 hours, Mean ± SEM; n = 15 syllables). This regressive effect of LMAN-RA terminal suppression on syllable maturity manifested only on laser-targeted renditions and was significant compared to laser shuffled data ( [Extended Data Fig. 4a,b](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#F8)). Suppression of LMAN-RA terminals also reduced variability measured across all acoustic features ( [Extended Data Fig. 4c](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#F8)), consistent with prior experiments[28](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#ref-28), [29](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.01.20.699515v1.full#ref-29).
